CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro, Related Work, Method, Experiments, Conclusion) and clearly states main contributions early (Intro lists 5 properties). Notation is introduced in a dedicated subsection and used consistently through the equations, making the method and corruption-based regularization understandable. Figures/tables are referred to effectively (e.g., Figure 1, Tables 1–6, t-SNE visualizations) though the provided text contains extraction/formatting artifacts and some tables/figures are not shown or are garbled. Writing is generally clear and concise, but the manuscript has occasional duplicated lines, LaTeX remnants and minor grammatical/formatting issues that hinder smooth reading.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes Doc2VecC which enforces that a document is represented as the average of word embeddings during learning and augments this with an unbiased mask-out corruption that yields a data-dependent regularizer (sec.1, sec.3). These ideas are a straightforward combination of existing components—averaging-based document representations (Huang et al. 2012; post-hoc Word2Vec averaging), denoising/dropout-style corruption and analysis (Vincent et al. 2008; Wager et al. 2013), and contrasts with Paragraph Vectors (Le & Mikolov) that learn per-document vectors—so the contribution is a useful, non-trivial engineering/analytical refinement rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates itself from prior work but the novelty is largely incremental (a focused combination and analysis) rather than highly original.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are supported: the Taylor-expansion derivation of a data-dependent regularizer from the corruption model is mathematically plausible (sec.3.1) and the experiments (IMDB sentiment, word-analogy, Wikipedia classification, SICK) show consistent empirical gains over reasonable baselines (Word2Vec+AVG/IDF, Doc2Vec, DAE, Skip‑thought). Minor technical issues remain: the second-order Taylor approximation and its application to the negative-sampling objective are not fully justified (assumptions and error bounds omitted), and some experimental protocol choices reduce clarity (representation learning sometimes used the test set, differing training regimes for Skip‑thought, no statistical significance reporting). These are shortcomings that do not invalidate the main conclusions but warrant further clarification or ablation.
